clean_story:
A lost folk album. A life unraveled.

Jackson C. Frank’s name comes from his full title—Blues Run the Game—the only album he ever recorded. Like a single match struck in a storm, it flared bright and vanished.

At 21, he survived a school furnace explosion that killed 15 classmates. The insurance payout funded his 1965 UK trip, where a 17-year-old Paul Simon heard him play in a London club. Simon later covered Blues Run the Game, but Frank’s own version—a voice like sandpaper on silk, guitar tuned to an aching minor key—stayed obscure. His songs (e.g., Marlene, Can’t Get Away From My Love) were sparse, precise: three chords, no filler, lyrics that cut like "a knife left in the rain."

By the 1970s, schizophrenia and homelessness erased his career. He busked for change, slept on friends’ couches, and in 1996, died of cardiac arrest in a Queens hospital. His album, pressed in 500 copies, now sells for $2,000+. Nick Drake cited him; John Mayer called Blues Run the Game "the saddest song ever."

Three related subjects are one-hit music legends, folk’s dark undercurrents, and art born from trauma.
